content.problemStatement:
Ravi has a tree with NNN nodes, with its root being node 111. The iii-th node of this tree has PiP_iPi​ points.

Ravi is initially standing on node 111 of this tree. He'd like to visit several nodes of this tree and collect points. To do this, he can move through the edges of the tree.

That is, if Ravi is standing on a node uuu, he can pick a neighbor of uuu and move to it. This can be done however many times he likes; and it is allowed to visit nodes multiple times.

Ravi has a score SSS, which initially equals P1P_1P1​.

The first time that Ravi visits a vertex uuu, he **must** add PuP_uPu​ to his score. Note that node 111 is considered visited, since Ravi starts there.

Ravi's objective is to maximize SSS.

Ravi, however, has been cursed. Whenever he moves from a vertex uuu to its child vvv, the point value of every node in the subtree†^\dagger† of vvv decreases by 111.

This decrease happens before Ravi reaches vvv.

Malay decides to help Ravi a little, and can lift Ravi's curse for **exactly one node**.

That is, Ravi can choose a single node xxx (2≤x≤N2 \leq x \leq N2≤x≤N), and Malay will ensure that Ravi's curse doesn't activate when entering node xxx from its parent (meaning the values within the subtree of xxx will be unchanged).

Ravi wants to maximize his score, but doesn't know how best to make use of his one-time-use boon; and so turns to you for help.

Can you tell Ravi what his maximum possible score is if he chooses to lift the curse for node xxx, for each xxx from 222 to NNN?

†^\dagger† For a tree rooted at node 111, xxx lies in the subtree of yyy if and only if the (unique) path from node 111 to node xxx contains node yyy.

In particular, xxx is contained in its own subtree.

**Note:**


Ravi's curse activates only when moving from a vertex to its child - however, Ravi is allowed to move from a vertex to its parent if he wants to (which won't activate the curse).
Ravi does not need to visit every vertex of the tree: he only wants to maximize his score.

Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the tree.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​ — the initial point values of the nodes.
The next N−1N-1N−1 lines describe the tree. Each of them contains two space-separated integers uuu and vvv, denoting an edge between nodes uuu and vvv in the tree.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line N−1N-1N−1 space-separated integers A2,A3,…,ANA_2, A_3, \ldots, A_NA2​,A3​,…,AN​.

AiA_iAi​ should denote the answer if the curse on node iii is lifted.
constraints: The input edges describe a tree.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 3⋅1053\cdot 10^53⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Let's look at what happens when the curse is relaxed for node 333.

Initially, V=[4,3,2,3,3,−5]V = [4, 3, 2, 3, 3, -5]V=[4,3,2,3,3,−5].

Ravi starts at 111, with a score of V1=4V_1 = 4V1​=4.


First, Ravi will move to node 222, which is a child of 111. The curse activates; and everything in the subtree of 222 (nodes 222 and 444) will have their values reduced by 111.
So, V=[4,2,2,2,3,−5]V = [4, 2, 2, 2, 3, -5]V=[4,2,2,2,3,−5] now.

Ravi is visiting 222 for the first time, so he adds V2=2V_2 = 2V2​=2 to his score; which is now 666.


Next, Ravi visits 444, which is the child of 222. The curse activates, so V=[4,2,2,1,3,−5]V = [4, 2, 2, 1, 3, -5]V=[4,2,2,1,3,−5].

Ravi is visiting 444 for the first time, so he adds V4=1V_4 = 1V4​=1 to his score; which is now 777.


Ravi moves up to 222. Since he's moving up, the curse doesn't activate.

222 was visited before, so Ravi doesn't add its value to his score.


Ravi moves up to 111. The curse doesn't activate; and he doesn't add V1V_1V1​ to his score.
Ravi moves down to 333. Since the curse was lifted on 333, VVV doesn't change.

333 is being visited for the first time, so V3=2V_3 = 2V3​=2 is added to the score; which is now 999.


Ravi moves down to 555. The curse activates, and V5V_5V5​ becomes 222. This is added to the score, which is now 111111.
Ravi will stop here, since visiting node 666 is not optimal.

Thus, A3=11A_3 = 11A3​=11 is his optimal score if the curse is lifted on node 333.
Note that A3A_3A3​ is the second number in the output, since the first is A2A_2A2​.
Test case 222: The values on the nodes are [−2,1,1][-2, 1, 1][−2,1,1].

Suppose the curse is relaxed on node 222. Ravi's optimal movement is:

Start at node 111, with a score of V1=−2V_1 = -2V1​=−2.
Move to node 222. Since the curse is relaxed, its value won't reduce, and he adds V2=1V_2 = 1V2​=1 to his score; for a total of −1-1−1. This is optimal.


If the curse is relaxed on node 333 instead, Ravi will move 1→31\to 31→3 instead, with his score being −2+1=−1-2 + 1 = -1−2+1=−1 again.
